title: How to Determine Methane Production Rate (MPR)
author: hussain, zahid
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
How to Determine Methane Production Rate (MPR)  
  A Practical and Analytical Overview  


  1. Introduction  
 Methane production rate (MPR) is a key performance indicator in  anaerobic digestion ,  biogas systems , and  bioenergy biotechnology . It reflects the efficiency of microbial conversion of organic matter into methane (CH₄). Calculating MPR accurately requires standardized gas measurements, normalization to temperature and pressure, and often normalization to reactor or substrate characteristics. 


  2. Basic Formula for Methane Production Rate  
    MPR=VCH4VR×t\text{MPR} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{V_R \times t}     MPR   =         V    R      ×  t  V     C  H  4                    
 Where: 
 
 
   VCH4V_{CH_4}     V        C  H  4                = Volume of methane produced (L or mL) 
 
 
   VRV_R     V       R             = Reactor working volume (L) 
 
 
   tt    t     = Time period (days or hours) 
 
 
  Example:  If 250 mL of methane is produced in 2 days from a 1 L reactor: 
    MPR=2501×2=125 mL CH4/L/day\text{MPR} = \frac{250}{1 \times 2} = 125 \text{ mL CH}_4\text{/L/day}     MPR   =        1 × 2250        =    125    mL CH       4           /L/day       


  3. Correction to Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)  
 Gas volume is affected by temperature and pressure. To compare data globally, convert the measured gas volume to STP (0°C, 101.325 kPa): 
    VCH4,STP=VCH4×(P−Pw)101.325×273.15273.15+TV_{CH_4, STP} = V_{CH_4} \times \frac{(P - P_w)}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{273.15 + T}     V        C  H  4    ,  STP           =     V        C  H  4             ×        101.325 (  P  −  P    w      )         ×        273.15 +  T 273.15            
 Where: 
 
 
   PP    P     = Atmospheric pressure (kPa) 
 
 
   PwP_w     P       w             = Water vapor pressure (kPa, dependent on temperature) 
 
 
   TT    T     = Measured temperature (°C) 
 
 
  Example:  At 30°C (vapor pressure ≈ 4.24 kPa) and 101 kPa total pressure: 
    VCH4,STP=500×(101−4.24)101.325×273.15303.15=431.3 mLV_{CH_4, STP} = 500 \times \frac{(101 - 4.24)}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{303.15} = 431.3 \text{ mL}     V        C  H  4    ,  STP           =    500  ×        101.325 ( 101 − 4.24 )         ×        303.15273.15        =    431.3    mL       


  4. Methane Volume from Biogas Composition  
 If only total biogas volume and methane content are known: 
    VCH4=Vbiogas×%CH4100V_{CH_4} = V_{biogas} \times \frac{\%CH_4}{100}     V        C  H  4             =     V        bi  o  g  a  s           ×        100% C  H    4                 
  Example:  If 1.5 L of biogas contains 60% methane: 
    VCH4=1.5×0.6=0.9 L CH4V_{CH_4} = 1.5 \times 0.6 = 0.9 \text{ L CH}_4     V        C  H  4             =    1.5  ×    0.6  =    0.9    L CH       4              
 Then apply the MPR formula to find daily production per reactor volume. 


  5. Specific Methane Production (SMP)  
 This measures methane yield per unit of substrate (commonly volatile solids): 
    SMP=VCH4VSadded\text{SMP} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{VS_{added}}     SMP   =         V  S     a  dd  e  d       V     C  H  4                    
 Where   VSaddedVS_{added}    V   S        a  dd  e  d              = grams of volatile solids added or degraded. 
  Example:  If 800 mL CH₄ is produced from 2 g VS: 
    SMP=8002=400 mL CH4/g VS\text{SMP} = \frac{800}{2} = 400 \text{ mL CH}_4\text{/g VS}     SMP   =        2800        =    400    mL CH       4           /g VS       


  6. Methane Production Rate from Time-Series Data  
 If gas production is measured daily, the MPR can be derived from the  slope of the cumulative methane curve : 
    MPR=dVCH4dt\text{MPR} = \frac{dV_{CH_4}}{dt}     MPR   =         d  t  d  V     C  H  4                    
 Use software (Excel, Origin, MATLAB) to find the slope of cumulative methane vs. time curve. 


  7. COD-Based Methane Production Estimation  
 Theoretical conversion assumes that  1 g COD removed produces 0.35 L CH₄ at STP : 
    VCH4,theoretical=0.35×(COD removed in g)V_{CH_4, \text{theoretical}} = 0.35 \times (\text{COD removed in g})     V        C  H  4    ,  theoretical           =    0.35  ×    (   COD removed in g   )      
  Example:  If 1000 mg COD is removed: 
    VCH4=0.35×1=0.35 L CH4V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 1 = 0.35 \text{ L CH}_4     V        C  H  4             =    0.35  ×    1  =    0.35    L CH       4              


  8. Common Units for Reporting MPR  
 
 
 
 
 
 Unit 
 Meaning 
 Typical Use 
 
 
 
 
 mL CH₄/L/day 
 Methane per reactor volume per day 
 Lab-scale digesters 
 
 
 L CH₄/g VS 
 Methane per volatile solids fed 
 Substrate comparison 
 
 
 L CH₄/g COD 
 Methane per COD removed 
 Wastewater treatment 
 
 
 m³ CH₄/m³/day 
 Industrial-scale digesters 
 Process control 
 
 
 
 
 


  9. Worked Example  
 
 
 
 
 
 Parameter 
 Symbol 
 Value 
 
 
 
 
 Total biogas produced 
   VbiogasV_{biogas}     V        bi  o  g  a  s              
 1.2 L 
 
 
 Methane fraction 
   %CH_4   
 65% 
 
 
 Temperature 
   TT    T     
 30°C 
 
 
 Pressure 
   PP    P     
 101 kPa 
 
 
 Reactor volume 
   VRV_R     V       R             
 1 L 
 
 
 Time 
   tt    t     
 2 days 
 
 
 
 
 
  Step 1:  
    VCH4=1.2×0.65=0.78 LV_{CH_4} = 1.2 \times 0.65 = 0.78 \text{ L}     V        C  H  4             =    1.2  ×    0.65  =    0.78    L       
  Step 2 (STP correction):  
    VCH4,STP=0.78×(101−4.24)101.325×273.15303.15=0.67 LV_{CH_4, STP} = 0.78 \times \frac{(101 - 4.24)}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{303.15} = 0.67 \text{ L}     V        C  H  4    ,  STP           =    0.78  ×        101.325 ( 101 − 4.24 )         ×        303.15273.15        =    0.67    L       
  Step 3 (Rate):  
    MPR=0.671×2=0.335 L CH4/L/day\text{MPR} = \frac{0.67}{1 \times 2} = 0.335 \text{ L CH}_4\text{/L/day}     MPR   =        1 × 20.67        =    0.335    L CH       4           /L/day       


  10. Summary Table  
 
 
 
 
 
 Equation 
 Purpose 
 Reference 
 
 
 
 
   MPR=VCH4/(VR×t)\text{MPR} = V_{CH_4}/(V_R \times t)     MPR   =     V        C  H  4             /  (   V       R          ×    t  )     
 Basic methane rate 
 Angelidaki & Sanders (2004) 
 
 
   VCH4,STP=VCH4×(P−Pw)101.325×273.15273.15+TV_{CH_4, STP} = V_{CH_4} \times \frac{(P - P_w)}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{273.15 + T}     V        C  H  4    ,  STP           =     V        C  H  4             ×          101.325     (  P  −  P    w      )           ×          273.15 +  T     273.15             
 STP correction 
 APHA (2017) 
 
 
   VCH4=Vbiogas×%CH4/100V_{CH_4} = V_{biogas} \times \%CH_4/100     V        C  H  4             =     V        bi  o  g  a  s           ×    %  C   H       4          /100     
 Methane fraction 
 Hansen et al. (2004) 
 
 
   SMP=VCH4/VSadded\text{SMP} = V_{CH_4}/VS_{added}     SMP   =     V        C  H  4             /  V   S        a  dd  e  d              
 Specific methane yield 
 Angelidaki et al. (2009) 
 
 
   VCH4=0.35×COD removed (g)V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times \text{COD removed (g)}     V        C  H  4             =    0.35  ×     COD removed (g)      
 Theoretical yield 
 Metcalf & Eddy (2014) 
 
 
 
 
 


  11. References  
 
 
  Angelidaki, I., & Sanders, W. (2004).  “Assessment of the anaerobic biodegradability of macropollutants.”  Reviews in Environmental Science and Bio/Technology , 3(2), 117–129. 
 
 
  Angelidaki, I., et al. (2009).  “Defining the biomethane potential (BMP) of solid organic wastes and energy crops: A proposed protocol.”  Water Science and Technology , 59(5), 927–934. 
 
 
  APHA (2017).   Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater , 23rd Edition. American Public Health Association. 
 
 
  Metcalf & Eddy, Inc. (2014).   Wastewater Engineering: Treatment and Resource Recovery , 5th Edition. McGraw-Hill Education. 
 
 
  Hansen, T. L., et al. (2004).  “Method for determination of methane potentials of solid organic waste.”  Waste Management , 24(4), 393–400. 
 
 
  Batstone, D. J., et al. (2002).  “Anaerobic digestion model No. 1 (ADM1).”  Water Science and Technology , 45(10), 65–73. 
 
 
  Labatut, R. A., et al. (2011).  “Evaluation of laboratory protocols for measuring biogas potential of organic waste.”  Environmental Technology , 32(8), 887–898.
